notes.title: Sets, Relations and Functions — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Foundation of mathematics: sets, types of relations and functions.

## 1. Sets
- Operations: union, intersection, difference, complement. De Morgan's laws: (A∪B)' = A'∩B', (A∩B)' = A'∪B'.
- n(A∪B) = n(A) + n(B) − n(A∩B). Power set has 2ⁿ subsets.

## 2. Relations
- A relation R ⊆ A×B. Types: reflexive (aRa), symmetric (aRb⇒bRa), transitive, **equivalence** (all three).

## 3. Functions
- Types: one-one (injective), onto (surjective), bijective.
- Domain, codomain, range. Even f(−x)=f(x); odd f(−x)=−f(x).
- Composite (fog)(x) = f(g(x)); inverse exists only for bijections.

## 4. Standard Functions
- Modulus, greatest integer [x], signum, exponential, logarithmic.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- A function must assign exactly one image to each domain element.
- Equivalence relation needs reflexive + symmetric + transitive.
- Inverse exists only if the function is bijective.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- n(A∪B) formula; 2ⁿ subsets.
- Equivalence relation conditions.
- One-one/onto/bijection.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For JEE, master finding domain/range of composite functions and checking injectivity/surjectivity.

notes.title: Trigonometric Functions — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Trigonometric ratios, identities and equations — a JEE staple.

## 1. Basics
- Radian–degree: π rad = 180°. sin²θ + cos²θ = 1; 1+tan²θ = sec²θ; 1+cot²θ = csc²θ.

## 2. Compound & Multiple Angles
- sin(A±B) = sinA cosB ± cosA sinB; cos(A±B) = cosA cosB ∓ sinA sinB.
- sin2A = 2 sinA cosA; cos2A = 1 − 2sin²A = 2cos²A − 1; tan2A = 2tanA/(1−tan²A).

## 3. Transformations
- Sum-to-product and product-to-sum formulae.

## 4. Trigonometric Equations
- General solutions: sinθ = sinα ⇒ θ = nπ + (−1)ⁿα; cosθ = cosα ⇒ θ = 2nπ ± α; tanθ = tanα ⇒ θ = nπ + α.

## 5. Properties of Triangles
- Sine rule a/sinA = 2R; cosine rule a² = b² + c² − 2bc cosA.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Always write the GENERAL solution for trig equations (include n).
- Check the domain when squaring trig equations (extraneous roots).
- Sign of ratios depends on the quadrant (ASTC rule).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Pythagorean & compound-angle identities.
- General solutions of sin/cos/tan equations.
- Sine & cosine rules.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Memorise the general-solution formulae — JEE trig equation problems hinge on them.

notes.title: Complex Numbers & Quadratic Equations — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Complex number algebra, geometry and quadratic theory.

## 1. Complex Numbers
- z = a + ib; i² = −1. Modulus |z| = √(a²+b²); argument arg(z).
- Conjugate z̄ = a − ib; |z|² = z·z̄.
- **Polar form:** z = r(cosθ + i sinθ); Euler: z = re^{iθ}.
- **De Moivre:** (cosθ + i sinθ)ⁿ = cos nθ + i sin nθ.

## 2. Properties
- |z₁z₂| = |z₁||z₂|; arg(z₁z₂) = arg z₁ + arg z₂.
- Cube roots of unity: 1, ω, ω²; 1 + ω + ω² = 0, ω³ = 1.

## 3. Quadratic Equations
- Roots of ax² + bx + c = 0: x = (−b ± √D)/2a; discriminant D = b² − 4ac.
- D > 0 (real distinct), D = 0 (equal), D < 0 (complex conjugate roots).
- Sum of roots = −b/a; product = c/a.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- For real coefficients, complex roots occur in conjugate pairs.
- 1 + ω + ω² = 0 simplifies many expressions.
- Don't forget the modulus is always non-negative.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- |z|, arg, polar form, De Moivre.
- Cube roots of unity properties.
- D and root relationships.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Use cube roots of unity (ω) tricks and conjugate-root property — recurring JEE shortcuts.

notes.title: Permutations and Combinations — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Counting principles, arrangements and selections.

## 1. Fundamental Principle of Counting
- Multiplication (AND) and addition (OR) rules.

## 2. Permutations (order matters)
- ⁿPᵣ = n!/(n−r)!. Arrangements of n distinct = n!.
- With repetition allowed: nʳ. Circular permutations: (n−1)!.
- Identical objects: n!/(p!q!r!).

## 3. Combinations (order does not matter)
- ⁿCᵣ = n!/(r!(n−r)!). ⁿCᵣ = ⁿC₍ₙ₋ᵣ₎; ⁿCᵣ + ⁿC₍ᵣ₋₁₎ = ⁿ⁺¹Cᵣ.

## 4. Applications
- Distribution, selection with constraints, grouping problems.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Use permutation when order matters, combination when it doesn't.
- Circular arrangements: fix one position → (n−1)!.
- Watch for identical objects (divide by factorials).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- ⁿPᵣ = n!/(n−r)!; ⁿCᵣ = n!/(r!(n−r)!).
- Circular = (n−1)!.
- Pascal's identity.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Decide order-matters vs not FIRST; that single decision picks P or C correctly.

notes.title: Binomial Theorem — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Expansion of (a+b)ⁿ and its applications.

## 1. Binomial Expansion
- (a+b)ⁿ = Σ ⁿCᵣ aⁿ⁻ʳ bʳ (r = 0 to n).
- General term: T₍ᵣ₊₁₎ = ⁿCᵣ aⁿ⁻ʳ bʳ.

## 2. Key Results
- Number of terms = n+1. Middle term: if n even, ((n/2)+1)th; if n odd, two middle terms.
- Greatest coefficient; term independent of x (put exponent = 0).

## 3. Properties
- Sum of coefficients: put variables = 1. ⁿC₀ + ⁿC₁ + … + ⁿCₙ = 2ⁿ.
- Sum of even-indexed = sum of odd-indexed = 2ⁿ⁻¹.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- General term index: T₍ᵣ₊₁₎ (r starts at 0).
- For term independent of x, set the power of x to zero.
- Negative/fractional index needs the infinite series form (|x|<1).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- General term T₍ᵣ₊₁₎ = ⁿCᵣ aⁿ⁻ʳ bʳ.
- Sum of coefficients = 2ⁿ.
- Middle term rules.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Most JEE questions ask for a specific/independent term — use the general term and solve for r.

notes.title: Sequences and Series — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Arithmetic, geometric and harmonic progressions and special series.

## 1. Arithmetic Progression (AP)
- nth term: aₙ = a + (n−1)d. Sum: Sₙ = n/2[2a + (n−1)d] = n/2(a + l).
- AM of a,b = (a+b)/2.

## 2. Geometric Progression (GP)
- nth term: aₙ = arⁿ⁻¹. Sum: Sₙ = a(rⁿ−1)/(r−1). Infinite sum (|r|<1): S∞ = a/(1−r).
- GM of a,b = √(ab).

## 3. Harmonic Progression & Means
- HM of a,b = 2ab/(a+b). Relation: AM ≥ GM ≥ HM; GM² = AM × HM.

## 4. Special Series
- Σn = n(n+1)/2; Σn² = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6; Σn³ = [n(n+1)/2]².

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- AM ≥ GM ≥ HM (equality only when all terms equal).
- Infinite GP sum needs |r| < 1.
- GM² = AM × HM is a common shortcut.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- AP/GP nth term & sum formulae.
- AM ≥ GM ≥ HM.
- Σn, Σn², Σn³ formulae.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
The AM–GM inequality solves many JEE maxima/minima problems quickly.

notes.title: Straight Lines — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Coordinate geometry of lines: forms, slopes, distances and angles.

## 1. Slope & Forms
- Slope m = tanθ = (y₂−y₁)/(x₂−x₁).
- Forms: slope-intercept y = mx + c; point-slope y − y₁ = m(x − x₁); two-point; intercept x/a + y/b = 1; normal form.

## 2. Angle & Conditions
- Angle between lines: tanθ = |(m₁−m₂)/(1+m₁m₂)|.
- Parallel: m₁ = m₂. Perpendicular: m₁m₂ = −1.

## 3. Distances
- Distance of point (x₁,y₁) from ax+by+c=0: |ax₁+by₁+c|/√(a²+b²).
- Distance between parallel lines: |c₁−c₂|/√(a²+b²).

## 4. Other
- Family of lines through intersection: L₁ + λL₂ = 0.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Perpendicular lines: product of slopes = −1 (vertical/horizontal are special cases).
- Use the modulus in the distance formula.
- A vertical line has an undefined slope.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Slope, all line forms.
- Angle, parallel & perpendicular conditions.
- Point-line & parallel-line distances.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Use the family of lines (L₁ + λL₂) for 'line through intersection' JEE problems.

notes.title: Conic Sections — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Circle, parabola, ellipse and hyperbola — geometry and standard equations.

## 1. Circle
- (x−h)² + (y−k)² = r². General: x²+y²+2gx+2fy+c=0, centre (−g,−f), radius √(g²+f²−c).

## 2. Parabola
- y² = 4ax: vertex (0,0), focus (a,0), directrix x=−a, latus rectum = 4a.

## 3. Ellipse
- x²/a² + y²/b² = 1 (a>b): foci (±ae,0), e = √(1−b²/a²) <1, latus rectum 2b²/a.

## 4. Hyperbola
- x²/a² − y²/b² = 1: e = √(1+b²/a²) >1; asymptotes y = ±(b/a)x.

## 5. Eccentricity Summary
- Circle e=0, parabola e=1, ellipse 0<e<1, hyperbola e>1.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Eccentricity value identifies the conic.
- Latus rectum: parabola 4a, ellipse/hyperbola 2b²/a.
- For ellipse e<1, hyperbola e>1.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Standard equations & key elements.
- Eccentricity ranges.
- Latus rectum formulae.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn focus, directrix, eccentricity and latus rectum for each conic — JEE asks these directly.

notes.title: Limits and Derivatives — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Introduction to calculus: limits, continuity and differentiation.

## 1. Limits
- lim(x→a) f(x). Standard: lim(x→0) sinx/x = 1; lim(x→0)(eˣ−1)/x = 1; lim(x→0)(1+x)^{1/x} = e.
- Indeterminate forms (0/0, ∞/∞) → factorise, rationalise, or use standard limits.

## 2. Derivatives (first principle)
- f'(x) = lim(h→0)[f(x+h)−f(x)]/h.

## 3. Standard Derivatives
- d/dx(xⁿ)=nxⁿ⁻¹; d/dx(sinx)=cosx; d/dx(cosx)=−sinx; d/dx(eˣ)=eˣ; d/dx(lnx)=1/x.

## 4. Rules
- Product, quotient, and chain rules.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- A limit exists only if LHL = RHL.
- Remember lim(x→0) sinx/x = 1 only when x is in radians.
- Apply the chain rule for composite functions.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Standard limits (sinx/x, (eˣ−1)/x).
- First-principle derivative.
- Product/quotient/chain rules.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn the standard limits cold — they convert most JEE limit problems into one step.

notes.title: Statistics — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Measures of central tendency and dispersion.

## 1. Central Tendency
- Mean, median, mode. Empirical relation: Mode = 3 Median − 2 Mean.

## 2. Dispersion
- Range, mean deviation, **variance** σ² and **standard deviation** σ.
- Variance = (Σxᵢ²/n) − (mean)².

## 3. Coefficient of Variation
- CV = (σ/mean) × 100; compares variability of data sets (lower CV = more consistent).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Standard deviation is the positive square root of variance.
- Variance is unaffected by adding a constant to all values; multiplied by k² if scaled by k.
- Lower CV means more consistency.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Mean, median, mode + empirical relation.
- Variance & SD formulae.
- CV for comparison.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Use variance = mean of squares − square of mean for quick computation.

notes.title: Probability — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Classical probability, addition/multiplication rules and conditional probability.

## 1. Basics
- P(E) = favourable/total outcomes; 0 ≤ P(E) ≤ 1. P(E') = 1 − P(E).

## 2. Addition & Multiplication
- P(A∪B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A∩B).
- Independent events: P(A∩B) = P(A)P(B).
- Mutually exclusive: P(A∩B) = 0.

## 3. Conditional Probability & Bayes
- P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B).
- **Bayes' theorem:** P(Eᵢ|A) = P(Eᵢ)P(A|Eᵢ)/ΣP(Eⱼ)P(A|Eⱼ).

## 4. Random Variable
- Expectation E(X) = Σ xᵢpᵢ. Binomial distribution: P(X=r) = ⁿCᵣ pʳq^{n−r}.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Mutually exclusive ≠ independent (they are different concepts).
- Use Bayes' theorem for 'reverse' conditional probability.
- Sum of all probabilities = 1.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Addition & multiplication rules.
- Conditional probability; Bayes.
- Binomial distribution.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Identify whether events are independent or mutually exclusive before applying formulae — a common JEE trap.
